title: The five-minute evening download
subtitle: A bedtime ritual: five minutes, one notebook page, every feeling out of the head and onto paper.
excerpt: A five-minute evening ritual that empties the day's accumulated feelings onto the page before bed. Same notebook each evening, same five minutes, no editing. Use on the wired nights when the body wants to sleep but the head is still narrating.
sourceNotes: Free-writing as a bedtime download is a public-domain practice with multiple lineages — the "morning pages" form documented by Julia Cameron (The Artist's Way, 1992) is the modern reference; the evening version goes back further into journaling traditions. The specific five-minute single-page form here is adapted from Day 6 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).

practice.whenToUse: Use in the half-hour before bed on a wired evening — body tired but head still narrating, accumulated feelings from the day refusing to settle.

content.text: A five-minute evening ritual that empties the day's accumulated feelings onto the page before bed. One notebook, one page, no editing. The practice is the steady writing; the page itself is for the practice, not for review.
A notebook kept for this purpose. A pen. A five-minute timer (a phone timer with the phone face-down, or a kitchen timer).
Sit somewhere you can stay for five minutes — bed, sofa, kitchen table. Open the notebook to a fresh page. Start the timer.

attrs.quote: I am ready to release today's accumulated feelings onto the page. I release it now. I release it now. I release it now.
I am ready to align with and allow rest after this page is done. I allow it now. I allow it now. I allow it now.

content.text: Write for five minutes. Everything that wants to come out. The list of unanswered emails, the conversation that didn't land right, the thing you forgot to do. Don't shape the writing. Don't worry about spelling. Don't stop the pen.
If you run out of things, write "I have run out" until something else arrives. Something else always arrives.
When the timer ends, finish the sentence and put the pen down. Don't read back what you wrote. Close the notebook.

attrs.quote: What needed to come out is on the page. The night is mine.

content.text: Put the notebook on the bedside table. The ritual is closed.
If the writing wakes the mind up rather than settling it, switch to
instead. Some bodies settle through the page; others settle through the body.

Free-writing as a bedtime download is a public-domain practice with multiple working lineages. The "morning pages" form documented by Julia Cameron (The Artist's Way, 1992) is the modern reference for unedited free-writing; the evening version is older, traceable through diaristic traditions in England, France, and Japan across the eighteenth and nineteenth centuries. The five-minute single-page form used here is adapted from Day 6 of SLEEP: A 30-Day Tapping Intensive (Rebecca J Page, 2025).
